objections.description: The `/{path:path}` catch-all route will swallow ALL non-API GET requests including health checks, metrics endpoints, favicon.ico, robots.txt, and any future backend routes added outside the `/api/` prefix. The guard only checks `api/` and `assets/` prefixes — any backend route like `/health`, `/ready`, `/metrics`, or webhook endpoints will silently return index.html instead of 404 or proper handler. This is a production outage waiting to happen when someone adds a non-prefixed backend route.

objections.location: Section 5.1-5.2 — Router choice unspecified / wouter mentioned once
objections.description: The spec mentions 'wouter hash router integration' exactly once in Router.jsx but never specifies: (1) whether this is hash-based routing (`/#/search`) or history-based (`/search`), (2) the SPA fallback in Section 2 assumes history-based routing (clean URLs), but there's no explicit decision, (3) wouter is never listed in package.json dependencies, (4) hash routing would make the entire Section 2 SPA catch-all unnecessary since all routes would be under `/#/`. This is an architectural decision that cascades into backend serving, link sharing, SEO (if relevant), and browser back-button behavior. It must be explicitly decided and its implications documented.
objections.suggested_fix: Decide: use history-based routing with wouter's `<Router>` (requires the SPA catch-all). Add `wouter` to package.json. Document the decision explicitly in Section 1 or a new 'Routing Strategy' section. If choosing hash routing, simplify Section 2 by removing the catch-all entirely. Recommendation: history-based routing for premium UX (clean URLs, shareable links), which means the SPA catch-all is necessary but needs the fix from the previous objection.

objections.description: The API client is described as a 'single fetch abstraction' but has zero specification of: error handling strategy (what happens on 401, 403, 500, network failure, timeout?), request cancellation via AbortController (critical for search-as-you-type to avoid stale responses overwriting fresh ones), retry policy, response type validation, or how loading/error states propagate to consuming hooks. The hooks (useSearch, useDocument, useDashboardData) are listed but their state management pattern is unspecified. Without this, every feature developer will invent their own error handling, leading to inconsistent UX on failures.

The spec states `--color-text-tertiary: #6E6E73` achieves 4.6:1 on `bg-primary (#0A0A0B)`. Actual computed contrast ratio for #6E6E73 on #0A0A0B is approximately 4.53:1 — barely scraping AA for normal text (≥4.5:1 required). This is within rounding tolerance but dangerously close. More critically, the spec says 'NOT used below 14px' but Tailwind's `text-xs` maps to 12px and `text-sm` to 14px. There's no enforcement mechanism — a developer can trivially use tertiary color at 12px and violate WCAG. Similarly, when tertiary text is used on `bg-secondary (#141416)` instead of `bg-primary`, the contrast drops below 4.5:1.
objections.suggested_fix: 1. Compute actual contrast ratios for tertiary on ALL background tokens, not just bg-primary. #6E6E73 on #141416 yields ~4.1:1 which FAILS AA. 2. Either bump tertiary to #7A7A80 (~5.0:1 on bg-secondary) or add an explicit constraint: 'tertiary text ONLY on bg-primary'. 3. Add a Tailwind plugin or lint rule that flags `text-xs` combined with tertiary color class usage. 4. Document the contrast matrix as a table: each text token × each background token = pass/fail.

objections.description: DataView is listed as 'Generic 4-state renderer' but its API, the four states, and how feature screens consume it are completely unspecified. This is the single most reused pattern in the app (every screen fetches data) and without a defined contract, each screen will implement loading/error/empty/success differently. The ScreenSkeleton variants (search, document, action, settings) are also referenced but unspecified.

SearchProvider wraps the entire app but its scope is undefined. If it holds search query, results, and autocomplete state globally, every keystroke in the search input will trigger re-renders across the entire component tree (including Dashboard, Document, etc.). If it's meant to persist search state across navigation (so users can go back to results), that's valid but must use state isolation (e.g., `useSyncExternalStore` or a ref-based store) to avoid cascade re-renders.
objections.suggested_fix: Either: (A) Scope SearchProvider to only wrap search-related routes, not the entire app. Or (B) Keep it global but specify that it uses a ref-based store pattern with selective subscriptions (like Zustand or a manual `useSyncExternalStore`) so only subscribed components re-render. Document which components subscribe to which slices.

objections.description: The 80KB initial bundle budget is aggressive (good) but there's no dependency inventory to validate feasibility. React 18 + ReactDOM is ~42KB gzipped. Wouter adds ~1.5KB. Tailwind (with purge) adds ~8-15KB depending on utility usage. That leaves ~20KB for all application code in the initial chunk. This is achievable but tight — and it means zero room for any utility library (date formatting, form validation, etc.). The spec should explicitly state: no runtime dependencies beyond React, ReactDOM, and wouter. Any utility needed must be hand-rolled or the budget must be revised.
objections.suggested_fix:
Add a 'Dependency Allowlist' section:

| Package | Size (gzip) | Justification |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| react | ~6KB | Runtime |
| react-dom | ~36KB | Runtime |
| wouter | ~1.5KB | Routing |
| **Total vendor** | **~44KB** | |
| **App budget** | **~36KB** | |

Explicitly state: 'No additional runtime dependencies without budget review. Date formatting uses Intl.DateTimeFormat. Form validation is hand-rolled. No lodash, no date-fns, no axios.'

objections.description: Screen enter/exit animations use translateX(16px/-16px) for directional navigation. This implies the app tracks navigation direction (forward vs back) to apply the correct animation class. Neither the Router nor any hook is specified to provide navigation direction state. With wouter, there's no built-in `action` property like React Router's `useNavigationType()`. Without this, the motion system for screen transitions is unimplementable as specified.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a `useNavigationDirection` hook that wraps wouter's `useLocation` and compares the current path against a ref of the previous path (or uses a route-index-based ordering) to determine forward/back direction. Alternatively, simplify: use `motion-fade-in` for all screen transitions and drop directional animation — it's subtle enough at 16px that users won't notice its absence, and it eliminates a class of bugs.

objections.location: Section 4.5 — useStaggerDelay missing from hook directory
objections.description: useStaggerDelay is defined inline in Section 4.5 and also listed in `shared/hooks/useStaggerDelay.js`, but the implementation shown is a plain function, not a hook (it doesn't use any React hooks). Naming it `useStaggerDelay` violates React's rules of hooks naming convention — linters will flag it and developers will assume it manages state.
objections.suggested_fix: Rename to `getStaggerDelay` or `calcStaggerDelay` since it's a pure function with no hook semantics. Or wrap it in `useMemo` if the intent is to memoize per-component: `function useStaggerDelay(index, total) { return useMemo(() => calcStaggerDelay(index, total), [index, total]); }`

objections.description: The inline script calls `document.querySelector('meta[name="theme-color"]')` but the `<meta name="theme-color">` tag is placed AFTER the script in the HTML. If the browser hasn't parsed the meta tag yet (it's below the script), the querySelector returns null and `.content = ...` throws a TypeError. This is a first-load crash in the critical path.

suggestions.description: Add a `/healthz` endpoint returning `{"status": "ok"}` before the SPA catch-all, regardless of which catch-all strategy is used. Every production deployment needs a health check endpoint that returns JSON, not HTML. This should be in the spec since the catch-all could mask its absence.

Add an explicit testing strategy section. The spec has zero mention of testing — no test runner (Vitest is the natural choice with Vite), no component test strategy, no accessibility testing tools (axe-core, testing-library queries), no integration test approach for API hooks. For a production frontend, this is a gap that will compound. At minimum: Vitest + @testing-library/react + axe-core for a11y assertions.

The focus ring uses `rgba(108, 92, 231, 0.6)` which may be invisible on surfaces that are close to the accent color (e.g., an accent-colored button). Add a focus ring style that uses a double-ring technique: `box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px var(--color-bg-primary), 0 0 0 4px var(--color-focus-ring-solid)` — the inner ring in the background color creates guaranteed contrast against any surface.

The ErrorBoundary wraps the entire app but there's only one level. A thrown error in DocumentScreen will take down the entire app and show a full-screen error state. Add per-route ErrorBoundaries inside each Suspense boundary so a feature crash is isolated to that screen, and the user can navigate away via TabBar/SideRail.

No mention of how the build is triggered in CI/CD or how `dist/` artifacts reach the server. Is `dist/` committed to the repo (don't), built in CI and copied, or built on the server? This determines whether `web_server.py` can assume `dist/` exists at startup. Add a build step to CI and a startup guard in web_server.py that fails fast with a clear error if `dist/` is missing.
